keyword: best herbal teas for stomach cramps
intro_1: Stomach cramps are painful, severe contraction of the abdominal muscles. According to the World Health Organization (WHO), it is estimated that over 250 million people worldwide suffer from stomach cramps every year. These cramps can significantly affect an individual's quality of life by causing discomfort and pain. The main causes of stomach cramps include gastroenteritis, food allergies, and constipation. They are also linked to other health problems such as irritable bowel syndrome, gallbladder disease, and ovarian cysts. In addition, stomach cramps can lead to malnutrition and dehydration if left untreated. It is important to seek medical attention for severe or persistent stomach cramps, especially in individuals with underlying health conditions.
intro_2: Herbal tea is an effective remedy to calm and soothe the stomach, providing relief from cramping and discomfort. Chamomile tea is particularly popular for its calming properties and can help to reduce the severity of stomach cramps. Peppermint tea can also provide relief by helping to relax the stomach muscles and alleviate pain. Ginger tea has anti-inflammatory properties that can help to reduce inflammation in the stomach and provide relief from cramping. Finally, licorice root tea can help to soothe an upset stomach and provide relief from cramps by reducing inflammation and promoting healing.
title: 10 best herbal teas for stomach cramps
intro: Herbal teas, particularly those made from chamomile, peppermint, and ginger, are known to provide relief from stomach cramps. This is because these herbs have anti-spasmodic properties that help relax the muscles of the gastrointestinal tract, reducing muscle spasms and consequently, cramps. Additionally, these herbs have anti-inflammatory effects that can help reduce inflammation in the stomach, further alleviating discomfort. Furthermore, herbal teas can help increase the production of digestive enzymes, promoting healthy digestion and reducing the likelihood of cramps. The soothing warmth of the tea can also help to relax the mind and body, reducing tension and stress that can exacerbate stomach cramps. However, it's important to note that while herbal teas can be helpful, they should not replace medical treatment, especially for severe or persistent stomach cramps.

remedies.remedy_desc: Chamomile tea helps with stomach cramps because it has anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties that help relax the smooth muscles of the intestines, reducing inflammation and spasms that cause pain. Its calming effects can also help alleviate stress and anxiety, which can exacerbate stomach cramps. Additionally, chamomile contains flavonoids, such as apigenin and luteolin, which have been shown to have antispasmodic and analgesic effects, further contributing to its stomach-soothing properties. Chamomile tea has been used as a traditional remedy for digestive issues for centuries, and its effectiveness is supported by some modern scientific studies. However, it is important to note that while chamomile tea can be helpful for occasional stomach cramps, it is not a substitute for medical treatment for serious or chronic digestive conditions.
remedy_parts: Flowers: Chamomile flowers are commonly used in medicinal teas for stomach cramps due to their anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties.
Leaves: The leaves of chamomile contain soothing agents that can help alleviate stomach cramps and promote digestive health.
Seeds: Chamomile seeds are rich in therapeutic compounds, and when used in tea, they can help relax the muscles of the digestive tract, reducing stomach cramps.
remedy_constituents: Apigenin: A flavonoid that has antispasmodic properties, helping to relax smooth muscle tissue in the digestive tract and alleviate stomach cramps.
Chamazulene: A chemical compound with anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce inflammation in the digestive tract and alleviate stomach cramps.
Alpha-Bisabolol: A terpene with anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties, helping to reduce inflammation and pain associated with stomach cramps.
Matricin: A precursor to chamazulene, matricin also has anti-inflammatory properties, aiding in the reduction of inflammation in the digestive tract.
Spiranes: A group of chemical compounds with antispasmodic properties that help relax the smooth muscle of the digestive tract and alleviate stomach cramps.
remedy_recipe: Boil 1 cup of water in a kettle or pot for 3-5 minutes.
Place 1 teaspoon of dried chamomile flowers in a tea infuser or teapot.
Pour the boiling water over the chamomile flowers and let steep for 5-10 minutes.
Remove the tea infuser or strain the tea to remove the flowers.
Allow the tea to cool slightly before drinking to soothe stomach cramps. Optional: add honey or lemon to taste.

remedies.remedy_desc: Ginger tea helps with stomach cramps because it contains gingerol, a compound with anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties that help relax the muscles in the gut lining, reducing contractions and subsequently alleviating pain. Furthermore, ginger has been shown to stimulate digestion, which can help move food through the gastrointestinal tract more efficiently, reducing the likelihood of cramping. Additionally, ginger tea can help regulate stomach acid production, preventing acid-related discomfort and potential cramping. Moreover, its soothing warmth may provide additional relief by increasing blood flow to the skin surface and relaxing tense abdominal muscles. Lastly, ginger's natural calming effects can help reduce anxiety and stress, which are often linked to digestive issues including stomach cramps.
remedy_parts: Rhizomes: The underground stem of ginger, known as rhizomes, is commonly used in medicinal tea due to its anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties that can help alleviate stomach cramps.
Roots: The ginger root contains bioactive compounds like gingerols and shogaols that can help relax the gastrointestinal muscles, providing relief from stomach cramps.
Leaves: Fresh ginger leaves are used in tea to treat stomach cramps, as they contain essential oils that can help soothe the digestive system and reduce inflammation.
Flowers: Ginger flowers are used in herbal teas for their calming effects on the stomach, as they contain anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties that can help alleviate stomach cramps.
Seeds: Ginger seeds contain oils that can help stimulate digestion, making them a valuable addition to medicinal tea for treating stomach cramps.
remedy_constituents: Gingerols: Gingerols, the most abundant compounds in ginger, have been shown to help relieve stomach cramps by relaxing the smooth muscle of the gastrointestinal tract, thus alleviating spasms and pain.

Shogaols: Shogaols, formed from gingerols when ginger is dried or cooked, also contribute to ginger's antispasmodic properties, helping to soothe stomach cramps by further reducing intestinal contractions and inflammation.

Paradols: Paradols, derived from gingerols and shogaols, exhibit anti-inflammatory and analgesic effects, which may help alleviate stomach cramps by reducing inflammation and pain in the gastrointestinal tract.

Zingerone: Zingerone, a compound formed when ginger is cooked or dried, has been found to inhibit gastrointestinal smooth muscle contraction and acid secretion, providing relief from stomach cramps and discomfort.
remedy_recipe: Peel and grate 1 inch of fresh ginger, let it steep in hot water for 10 minutes.
Boil 2 cups of water, then add grated ginger to release its medicinal properties.
Strain the ginger tea into a teapot or mug to remove ginger pieces.
Add honey and lemon juice to taste for soothing and vitamin C benefits.
Sip the warm ginger tea gradually to ease stomach cramps, letting it absorb completely.

remedies.remedy_desc: Peppermint tea helps with stomach cramps because it contains menthol, a compound with natural analgesic and antispasmodic properties that helps relax the gastrointestinal muscles, reducing painful spasms and cramping. Furthermore, it increases bile flow, which aids in digestion and speeds up the process of eliminating the contents of the stomach, thus alleviating discomfort. Additionally, peppermint tea is a carminative, which means it helps to reduce gas and bloating, providing further relief from stomach cramps. Its soothing and calming effects also help to reduce stress and anxiety, which can exacerbate digestive issues, making it an effective holistic remedy for stomach cramps. However, it's worth noting that excessive consumption of peppermint tea may cause heartburn or acid reflux in some individuals, so moderation is key.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Peppermint leaves are rich in menthol, a compound known to help relax the gastrointestinal muscles, thus alleviating stomach cramps.
Flowers: The flowers of peppermint contain flavonoids and volatile oils, which contribute to soothing stomach muscles and reducing discomfort caused by cramps.
Seeds: Peppermint seeds are a source of essential oils, including menthol, that can help ease stomach spasms and relieve cramping.
Stems: Peppermint stems contain therapeutic compounds, such as menthol and methyl salicylate, that can aid in reducing inflammation and relieving stomach cramps.
remedy_constituents: Menthol: Acts as a smooth muscle relaxant, helping to alleviate stomach cramps.
Methyl salicylate: Has anti-inflammatory properties, helping to reduce inflammation in the gastrointestinal tract and alleviate cramps.
Limonene: Helps to reduce gas and bloating, common causes of stomach cramps.
Menthone: Increases bile flow from the gallbladder, aiding in digestion and preventing cramps.
Pulegone: Inhibits the growth of harmful bacteria in the gut, which can cause stomach cramps.
remedy_recipe: In a teapot, combine 1 teaspoon of crushed dried peppermint leaves with 1 cup of boiling water.
Allow the tea to steep for 5-10 minutes to release the active ingredients.
Drink the tea while warm, up to three times a day for stomach cramp relief.

remedies.remedy_desc: Fennel tea helps with stomach cramps because it contains antispasmodic and carminative properties that help relax the gastrointestinal muscles, reducing muscle spasms and gas build-up. Furthermore, fennel has an antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effect, which may soothe the gastrointestinal tract and decrease inflammation. Its volatile oils, such as anethole, fenchone, and estragole, promote the production of digestive enzymes, enhancing the digestion process and alleviating symptoms of cramps. In addition, fennel tea can help stimulate the expulsion of gas, relieving bloating and discomfort in the abdomen. Overall, fennel tea's calming influence on the digestive system makes it an effective natural remedy for stomach cramps and related issues.
remedy_parts: Seeds: Fennel seeds are commonly used in medicinal tea for stomach cramps due to their carminative properties that help relieve bloating and gas.
Leaves: The leaves of the fennel plant are often used in tea to soothe stomach cramps, thanks to their antispasmodic and calming effects on the gastrointestinal tract.
Stems: Fennel stems can be used in medicinal tea to aid digestion and alleviate stomach cramps, as they contain volatile oils with anti-inflammatory and pain-relieving qualities.
Flowers: The cheery yellow flowers of the fennel plant contain bioactive compounds that can be used in tea to help reduce stomach cramp pain and inflammation.
remedy_constituents: Anethole: This compound has anti-spasmodic properties, which helps relax the smooth muscle of the gastrointestinal tract, providing relief from stomach cramps.
Fenchone: This compound also has anti-spasmodic and carminative properties, which help reduce gas and soothe abdominal pain, thereby alleviating stomach cramps.
Estragole: This component has anti-inflammatory and anti-oxidant properties, which can help decrease inflammation in the digestive tract and provide relief from stomach cramps.
Limonene: This compound has carminative properties, which help alleviate symptoms such as bloating, gas, and stomach cramps.
remedy_recipe: Chop: Slice 1 tablespoon of fresh fennel seeds or 1 teaspoon of dried fennel seeds.
Steep: Place the fennel seeds in a tea infuser or loose in a teapot and pour 1 cup of boiling water over them.
Strain: Remove the tea infuser or strain out the fennel seeds.
Serve: Enjoy your homemade fennel tea, preferably with a little honey to taste, up to three times a day as needed for stomach cramps.

remedies.remedy_desc: Licorice tea helps with stomach cramps because it contains substances called glycyrrhizic acid and flavonoids, which can help decrease inflammation and strengthen the mucous lining of the stomach. These compounds also promote the production of mucus, which can protect the stomach from acid and irritation. Furthermore, licorice tea may increase the secretion of digestive fluids and bile, improving overall digestion and reducing the likelihood of cramps. Additionally, it has antispasmodic properties, which means it can help relax the gastrointestinal muscles and alleviate painful spasms. However, it's important to note that excessive consumption of licorice tea can lead to side effects like high blood pressure and potassium depletion, so it's recommended to consult with a healthcare provider before using it as a regular remedy for stomach cramps.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the licorice plant are commonly used in medicinal teas for stomach cramps due to their anti-inflammatory and soothing properties.
Rhyzomes: The rhyzomes of the licorice plant are used in teas for stomach cramps as they contain glycyrrhizin, which can help with gastric inflammation.
Leaves: The leaves of the licorice plant are used in medicinal teas for stomach cramps because they contain flavonoids, which have anti-spasmodic properties.
Flowers: The flowers of the licorice plant are used less commonly, but they can be included in teas for stomach cramps due to their mild sedative effects, which can help relax the stomach muscles.
remedy_constituents: Glycyrrhizin: This compound is the primary active ingredient in licorice, and has anti-inflammatory properties that can help alleviate stomach cramps and pain.
Liquiritin: Liquiritin helps increase mucus secretion in the gastrointestinal tract, which can protect the stomach lining and reduce cramping.
Glabridin: Glabridin exhibits spasmolytic effects, meaning it can help relax the smooth muscle of the digestive tract and alleviate stomach cramps.
Liquiritigenin: Liquiritigenin has been shown to inhibit prostaglandin synthesis, which can reduce inflammation and help manage stomach cramps.
remedy_recipe: In a teapot, combine 1 teaspoon of dried licorice root with 8 ounces of boiling water.
Allow the mixture to steep for 10-15 minutes to extract the medicinal properties.
Sip the tea slowly, allowing it to coat the stomach and relieve cramps.

remedies.remedy_desc: Turmeric tea helps with stomach cramps because it contains curcumin, a compound with potent anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties. This compound inhibits the production of prostaglandins, which are hormone-like substances that cause the smooth muscle lining of the stomach to contract and can lead to cramps. Moreover, curcumin helps in reducing spasms of the gastrointestinal tract, further alleviating discomfort and pain. Additionally, its antioxidant properties aid in healing any damage to the stomach lining caused by inflammation, thereby preventing recurring cramps. Lastly, turmeric tea is a natural and caffeine-free alternative to over-the-counter medications, making it a gentler option for those with sensitive stomachs or digestive issues.
remedy_parts: Rhizomes: The underground stem of turmeric, rich in curcumin, is used for its anti-inflammatory properties to soothe stomach cramps.
Roots: Packed with antioxidants, turmeric roots help alleviate stomach discomfort and reduce muscle spasms causing cramps.
Leaves: Turmeric leaves have been used in traditional medicine to treat gastrointestinal issues, providing relief from stomach cramps.
remedy_constituents: Curcumin: This is the most active constituent in turmeric, known for its anti-inflammatory properties, which can help alleviate stomach cramps by reducing inflammation in the digestive tract.
Turmeric oil: This constituent contains aromatic compounds that can help relax the smooth muscles of the gastrointestinal tract, thereby reducing stomach cramps.
Curcuminoids: These are a group of antioxidants that can help protect the lining of the stomach, reducing the risk of stomach cramps caused by irritation or inflammation.
Volatile oils: These constituents have carminative properties, which can help relieve gas and bloating, common causes of stomach cramps.
Bisdesmosidic saponins: These compounds can help increase the secretion of digestive juices, improving digestion and reducing the risk of stomach cramps caused by indigestion.
remedy_recipe: In a saucepan, combine 1 cup of water and 1/2 teaspoon of grated fresh turmeric (or 1/4 teaspoon of dried turmeric) and bring to a boil.
Strain the tea into a cup, then stir in 1 teaspoon of honey and a squeeze of lemon juice.
Optional: Add a pinch of black pepper to increase turmeric's bioavailability.
Sip the tea slowly, allowing it to soothe your stomach cramps.

remedies.remedy_desc: Catnip tea helps with stomach cramps because it contains nepetalactone, a compound that has antispasmodic properties, which can help relax the smooth muscles in the gastrointestinal tract, reducing the intensity and frequency of stomach cramps. Additionally, catnip has carminative effects, which mean it can help expel gas from the stomach and intestines, further alleviating discomfort. Moreover, catnip has been shown to have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, which may help reduce inflammation in the digestive tract and promote healing. Furthermore, catnip tea can help soothe the nervous system, reducing stress and anxiety, which can sometimes exacerbate stomach cramps. However, it's important to note that while catnip tea may help alleviate symptoms of stomach cramps, it's not a substitute for medical treatment, especially for severe or persistent symptoms.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves of the catnip plant are commonly used in medicinal tea for stomach cramps due to their antispasmodic properties, which help to relax the smooth muscles of the gastrointestinal tract.
Flowers: Catnip flowers contain volatile oils that have been shown to have muscle relaxant and anti-inflammatory effects, making them useful for soothing stomach cramps.
Stems: While not as potent as the leaves and flowers, the stems of the catnip plant still contain therapeutic compounds that can help to alleviate stomach cramps and promote digestive health.
Roots: The roots of the catnip plant contain anti-inflammatory compounds that can help to reduce inflammation in the digestive tract, thereby alleviating stomach cramps.
remedy_constituents: Nepetalactone: This compound is known to have antispasmodic properties, which can help relax the smooth muscles in the stomach and reduce cramping.
Valepotriates: These iridoid glycosides have been found to have antispasmodic and analgesic effects, which can help alleviate pain and discomfort caused by stomach cramps.
Nepetalic acid: This organic acid has been shown to have anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce inflammation in the stomach and alleviate cramping.
Marrubiin: This compound has been found to have antispasmodic and carminative effects, which can help relieve gas and reduce muscle spasms in the gastrointestinal tract.
Rosmarinic acid: This phenolic acid has been shown to have anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic effects, which can help soothe and relax the muscles in the stomach and reduce cramping.
remedy_recipe: In a teapot, combine 1 teaspoon of dried catnip leaves with 8 ounces of boiling water.
Steep the catnip leaves for 5-10 minutes, allowing the water to absorb the medicinal properties.
Strain the tea to remove the leaves, leaving only the liquid.
Optional: Add honey or lemon to taste for improved flavor.
Sip the catnip tea slowly, allowing it to relieve stomach cramps over the course of 30 minutes.

remedies.remedy_desc: Dandelion tea helps with stomach cramps because it contains linolenic acid and several other anti-inflammatory compounds that can help reduce inflammation in the stomach lining, thereby alleviating pain and discomfort. Additionally, dandelion tea promotes digestion by increasing the production of stomach acid and bile, which helps break down food more efficiently, reducing the likelihood of cramps. The tea also acts as a diuretic, helping the body eliminate waste and toxins that may cause stomach irritation. Furthermore, dandelion tea is rich in vitamins and minerals, including magnesium, potassium, and calcium, which are essential for proper muscle function, including the muscles in the stomach. Finally, dandelion tea has been used in traditional medicine for centuries as a natural remedy for various gastrointestinal ailments, including stomach cramps, and its efficacy has been backed up by some scientific studies.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of dandelions are used in medicinal tea for stomach cramps due to their high concentration of antioxidants and anti-inflammatory properties.
Leaves: Dandelion leaves are often used in tea to alleviate stomach cramps, as they contain high levels of vitamins A and C, as well as minerals that promote digestion.
Flowers: Dandelion flowers are used in medicinal tea for stomach cramps because they have been shown to have antispasmodic properties, which can help relax muscles and alleviate pain.

Buds: The buds of dandelions are a less commonly used part, but they are still included in medicinal tea for stomach cramps because they contain high levels of antioxidants and have anti-inflammatory effects.
remedy_constituents: Taraxacin: Taraxacin, a bitter principle found in dandelion, helps stimulate the production of digestive juices, which can alleviate stomach cramps by improving digestion and nutrient absorption.
Taraxacerin: Taraxacerin, another bitter principle in dandelion, contributes to relieving stomach cramps by promoting the flow of bile from the gallbladder, aiding in fat digestion.
Inulin: Inulin, a type of dietary fiber present in dandelion, helps maintain a healthy gut microbiome, which can reduce stomach cramps caused by indigestion, bloating, and irregular bowel movements.

Phenolic acids: Dandelion contains phenolic acids, such as chlorogenic and caffeic acids, which have anti-inflammatory properties that can help alleviate stomach cramps and other gastrointestinal issues.
Sesquiterpene lactones: Sesquiterpene lactones, like taraxinic acid, found in dandelion have antispasmodic properties that can help relax gastrointestinal muscles, thus reducing stomach cramps.
remedy_recipe: Harvest: Pick 2-3 handfuls of fresh dandelion flowers from an organic and pesticide-free area, ensuring they are fully open on a sunny day.
Rinse: Thoroughly rinse the flowers under cold running water to remove any dirt or insects, then gently pat them dry.
Steep: Place the flowers in a pot and pour 4 cups of boiling water over them, letting them steep for 10-15 minutes to extract the medicinal properties.
Strain: Strain the mixture using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth, discarding the flowers, and pour the tea into a mug or teapot.
Serve: Drink the dandelion tea warm, up to three times a day, between meals, for soothing relief of stomach cramps and discomfort.

remedies.remedy_desc: Cinnamon tea helps with stomach cramps because it contains anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties that aid in reducing inflammation and pain in the gastrointestinal tract. The soothing warmth of the tea increases blood flow to the abdominal area, helping to relax the muscles and alleviate cramping. Cinnamon is also known for its calming effects on the stomach, making it an effective natural remedy for digestive issues such as bloating, gas, and indigestion. Furthermore, it has antispasmodic qualities which help in preventing and relieving smooth muscle spasms, thus providing relief from stomach cramps. Additionally, cinnamon tea's ability to stimulate the production of digestive enzymes promotes better nutrient absorption and speeds up the elimination process, reducing the likelihood of stomach cramps caused by slow digestion.
remedy_parts: Rhizomes: Cinnamon rhizomes are used in medicinal tea due to their rich supply of volatile oils, such as cinnamaldehyde, which can help relax the gastrointestinal muscles and alleviate stomach cramps.
Bark: The bark of cinnamon trees contains high amounts of antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds that can soothe the gastrointestinal lining and provide relief from stomach cramps.

Seeds: Cinnamon seeds are a great addition to medicinal tea because they contain a substance called eugenol, which has anti-spasmodic properties that can help reduce intestinal spasms and stomach cramps.
remedy_constituents: Cinnamaldehyde: This compound is the main active ingredient in cinnamon and gives it its distinctive smell and taste. It has anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties that can help alleviate stomach cramps.

Eugenol: This compound is found in smaller amounts in cinnamon, but still contributes to its medicinal properties. It has antispasmodic effects, which can help relax the muscles in the gastrointestinal tract and reduce stomach cramps.
Tannins: These compounds are found in the bark of the cinnamon tree and have astringent properties. They can help reduce inflammation and provide relief from stomach cramps.
remedy_recipe: In a pot, combine 1 cup of water and 1 cinnamon stick; bring to a boil, taking 3-5 minutes.
Reduce heat and let it simmer for 10-15 minutes to allow the cinnamon to release its properties.
Remove from heat, add 1 teaspoon of honey (or to taste) and a squeeze of lemon juice if desired.
Stir well to dissolve the honey, then strain the tea to remove cinnamon bits.
Sip the tea slowly, allowing it to warm your stomach and soothe cramps.

remedies.remedy_desc: Angelica tea helps with stomach cramps because it contains chemical components, such as angelic acid, that have been found to aid in relaxing the gastrointestinal muscles, thereby alleviating the contraction-induced pain in the abdomen. remedy_parts: Roots: The angelica root is commonly used in medicinal tea for stomach cramps due to its anti-spasmodic properties which help relax the smooth muscles of the digestive tract.
Seeds: The seeds of the angelica plant are used to make tea to alleviate stomach cramps as they contain compounds that help soothe the gastrointestinal system and reduce inflammation.
Leaves: Angelica leaves are often used in medicinal tea for stomach cramps as they are rich in volatile oils and antioxidants, which help relax the stomach muscles and reduce pain.

remedy_constituents: Alexandrin: This compound found in angelica acts as a natural antispasmodic, helping to relieve stomach cramps by relaxing the smooth muscles in the gut and reducing spasms.
Beta-phellandrene: This compound has anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties, which can help alleviate stomach cramps by reducing inflammation and pain in the gastrointestinal tract.
Limonene: With its antispasmodic and anti-inflammatory properties, limonene can help soothe stomach cramps by reducing muscle spasms and inflammation in the stomach.
Para-cymene: This compound exhibits antispasmodic and analgesic effects, making it useful in relieving stomach cramps by relaxing the gastrointestinal muscles and reducing pain.
Myrcene: Myrcene is known for its antispasmodic, anti-inflammatory, and analgesic properties, helping to alleviate stomach cramps by reducing muscle spasms, inflammation, and pain.
remedy_recipe: In a small pot, pour 1 cup of water and bring it to a boil, which takes approximately 2-3 minutes.
Add 1 teaspoon of dried angelica root to the boiling water, then reduce heat and let it simmer for 10 minutes.
Turn off the heat and strain the tea to remove the solid particles.
Add honey or lemon to taste, and stir until fully dissolved.
Drink the tea while warm, up to three times a day, for stomach cramp relief.
